topic: A student is accused of plagiarism
raw_output:
[story 1]

The fluorescent lights of the library hummed with an irritating monotony, mirroring the buzzing in my skull. I stared at the screen, the accusation glowing starkly on the screen—a word document flagged, a citation mismatch highlighted in angry red. "This is absurd," I muttered, my jaw tight enough to ache. I ran a hand through my already messy hair, the gesture sharp and jerky. Every carefully constructed sentence, every meticulously sourced paragraph, felt suddenly flimsy, exposed under the harsh scrutiny of this charge. I felt a deep, frustrating sense of unfairness settle in my chest, a heavy, suffocating weight. I looked up at the professor, whose expression was a mask of disappointment, and I could only manage a slow, deliberate blink, trying to keep the tremor out of my voice.

[story 2]

"Again?" I asked, the sound coming out higher pitched than I intended. My hands clenched into fists beneath the desk, the knuckles turning white. I could feel the heat rising in my neck, a flush spreading across my cheeks that I desperately tried to smother with a forced calm. It was the third time this week, the third time some invisible mechanism had decided my work was somehow fraudulent. I leaned back in my chair, the cheap plastic groaning under my weight, and let out a slow, almost silent breath that tasted like stale coffee. *Why can't they just see that I did the work?* The thought was a bitter, sharp thing. I picked up my pen, the plastic cold against my fingertips, and began to pace the small space between the shelves, a restless, caged energy vibrating just beneath my skin.

[story 3]

The air in the office felt thick, stagnant, like breathing underwater. I kept fiddling with the edge of my notebook, a nervous habit that betrayed the churning in my stomach. When the professor finally finished his recitation of the evidence, I felt a strange detachment, as if I were watching a poorly rehearsed play from the wings. My eyes flickered around the room, landing briefly on the framed degrees on the wall before snapping back to the professor’s impassive face. I didn't argue; the words wouldn't come out coherent. Instead, I simply folded my hands neatly on the table, the movement precise and overly controlled. A profound weariness washed over me, a total depletion of energy, leaving me feeling hollowed out and utterly defeated by the relentless cycle of scrutiny.
